kept.title: Subagents return envelope + summary + file path, not the full brief/critique through Main's context
kept.current_state: The researcher's JSON envelope carries a full report_md field (researcher.md line ~301) and the brief is echoed through Main's context; Q/A returns its full critique similarly. On long multi-cycle sessions Main's context accumulates every full brief and critique verbatim — the exact 'copying large outputs through conversation history' cost the multi-agent doc flags. (Write-first already persists these to disk, so the full text in the return is redundant.)
kept.proposed_change: Change the subagent return contract to return ONLY the JSON envelope: counts + gate_passed/verdict + the on-disk file path + a <=200-word executive summary. Drop report_md from the return. The full brief/critique still lives on disk (write-first unchanged); Main reads specific sections from the file on demand. Edit researcher.md and qa.md output contracts.
kept.grounding: Anthropic 'How we built our multi-agent research system' — 'implement artifact systems where specialized agents can create outputs that persist independently,' which reduces 'token overhead from copying large outputs through conversation history' (the subagent-output filesystem pattern) (https://www.anthropic.com/engineering/multi-agent-research-system).
kept.expected_benefit: Keeps Main's context lean across long sessions (compounds over dozens of cycles), preserving reasoning quality late in a session. Less text to flush at end-of-turn also directly lowers the subagent stall surface (stalls were observed at the final file-write / end-flush).
kept.risk: Low. Main occasionally needs to Read the file for a detail it would otherwise have had inline — a cheap, deterministic file read. Ensure the <=200-word summary carries gate_passed/verdict + violated_criteria so Main can branch without opening the file in the common case.
kept.fits_constraints: The 5 handoff files are untouched (still written on disk) — only what is echoed through context shrinks, so the durable-state backbone is preserved. No agent re-split, exactly-3 at L3.

verdict.reasoning:
GROUNDING VERIFIED REAL AND ON-POINT. Fetched the Anthropic "How we built our multi-agent research system" doc; it says verbatim: "implement artifact systems where specialized agents can create outputs that persist independently," "reduces token overhead from copying large outputs through conversation history," and decisively "Subagents call tools to store their work in external systems, then pass lightweight references back to the coordinator." The proposal's cited pattern (return path+envelope, not full text) is exactly what the doc prescribes. Not cargo-cult.

CONSTRAINTS CLEAN. Prompt-only edit to researcher.md + qa.md. Five handoff files untouched (write-first unchanged), no re-split, exactly-3 at L3, no self-eval change, local-only, $0 metered, zero infra.

BUT PREMISE IS FACTUALLY OFF, shrinking the delta: (1) report_md is NOT a full-text dump — in all ~45 real archived briefs it already holds a FILE PATH (e.g. "handoff/current/research_brief_55.3.md"), and no .py/.ts parses it (grep-confirmed). So literal "drop report_md" would delete the pointer the proposal wants to ADD; the instruction is imprecise. (2) Q/A's return is ALREADY envelope-shaped (qa.md single-JSON: ok/verdict/one-line reason/violated_criteria/violation_details); the full critique already lives only on disk. So "Q/A returns its full critique similarly" is wrong and that half is near-no-op. (3) The genuine cost — researcher's final message echoing the whole brief (up to 1500w/3500w) into Main's Task-result context — is real but rare-event (1 researcher + 1 Q/A per step) on a 1M-context model, and Main usually re-Reads most of the brief to write the contract's research-gate summary + references (Q/A checks this), so a Read re-persists it and most saving evaporates in the common path. (4) Stall-reduction tie-in is speculative: the 2026-07-11 stalls were on the disk write-first flush, which this change leaves intact.

VERDICT: real, correct, well-grounded, low-cost, but modest/uncertain benefit with an overstated current-state. KEEP as a cheap P3 hygiene tightening. Reframe the edit before codifying: keep report_md as the path (or rename brief_path), ADD a <=200-word summary carrying gate_passed/verdict + violated_criteria, and add an explicit "final message = envelope + summary only; do NOT echo the full brief" line — primarily on researcher.md (the Q/A half is essentially already done). Not "drop report_md."

kept.title: Make the Layer-2 evaluator honest: delete the fabricated spot-check stub and add structured output on evaluate_proposal
kept.current_state: evaluator_agent.py is the 'separation of generation from evaluation' gate (its own docstring headline) but: (1) _run_spot_checks (lines 496-516) RETURNS HARDCODED numbers (sharpe_2x_cost=1.02, regime_shift=0.95, param_sweep=0.99), and evaluate_with_spot_checks (lines 486-492) can flip a CONDITIONAL verdict to PASS on that fabricated data -- currently latent (only evaluate_proposal is on the live path at autonomous_loop.py:464; evaluate_with_spot_checks has no live caller) but a one-line wiring would put promote/reject decisions on fake evidence. (2) The default model is gemini-2.5-flash (line 89) despite the docstring saying 'Claude Sonnet' (line 84). (3) Verdict JSON is parsed with find('{')/rfind('}') (lines 414-457), forcing a conservative FAIL on any parse hiccup. Separately, autonomous_loop._generate_phase (autonomous_loop.py:415) returns MOCK backtest results, so this evaluator currently grades mock data.
kept.proposed_change: Delete _run_spot_checks + evaluate_with_spot_checks (or wire them to a real backtest_engine spot-check before any live use -- never return fabricated numbers). Add output_config.format (json_schema over the 5-score EvaluationResult) to _call_model so the verdict is guaranteed-valid instead of find('{')-parsed. Add a WARN log when the evaluator runs against mock generate results so mock-vs-real is never silently conflated. Align the docstring to the actual model.
kept.grounding: Anthropic structured outputs (https://platform.claude.com/docs/en/build-with-claude/structured-outputs) for the schema; 'Building Effective Agents' + multi-agent research post on evaluator independence and honest verification ('separating generation from evaluation is the strongest lever', cited in evaluator_agent.py:6 and multi_agent_orchestrator.py:21). Fabricated verification data directly violates that principle.
kept.expected_benefit: Removes a fabricated-evidence path that could rubber-stamp a bad strategy if ever wired live; makes the verdict schema-safe (no conservative-FAIL-on-parse noise); prevents silent mock/real conflation.
kept.risk: Low. Mostly deletes latent/dead code + adds a schema; behavior on the live evaluate_proposal path only becomes more reliable. If the team wants real spot-checks, that is a larger follow-up (wire backtest_engine) -- deletion is the safe minimum now.
kept.fits_constraints: Paper-only, no infra. Improves adversarial-verification honesty without adding per-ticker cost (evaluator fires per dev-cycle proposal, not per ticker).

verdict.reasoning:
KEEP but low-priority. All specific code claims verified true against source: _run_spot_checks (evaluator_agent.py:512-516) returns hardcoded 1.02/0.95/0.99; evaluate_with_spot_checks (487-489) unconditionally flips CONDITIONAL->PASS since the hardcoded 1.02 always clears the 0.90 gate (a genuine fabricated-evidence rubber-stamp); docstring "Claude Sonnet" (l.84) contradicts default gemini-2.5-flash (l.89); find('{')/rfind('}') parse + conservative-FAIL (416-457) confirmed; _generate_phase returns mock (autonomous_loop.py:415) confirmed.

No binding-constraint violation: this is an in-app evaluator, not the L3 Claude Code harness MAS -- doesn't touch the exactly-3-agents count, Q/A independence, self-eval ban, or the 5-file protocol. No new metered cost (same Gemini call, fires per-proposal not per-ticker), paper/local-only.

Why WEAK/P3, not P1/P2: leverage is near-zero today because the target is doubly latent. (1) evaluate_with_spot_checks/_run_spot_checks have NO caller anywhere in the repo. (2) The host AutonomousLoopOrchestrator (backend/autonomous_loop.py) is itself not production-wired -- only tests and the manual scripts/harness/run_autonomous_loop.py exercise it, and it grades MOCK backtest data. The LIVE paper-trading loop is a different module (backend/services/autonomous_loop.py::run_daily_cycle, used by backend/api/paper_trading.py) that does not use EvaluatorAgent at all. So "could rubber-stamp a bad strategy if ever wired live" is two wiring mistakes away from mattering -- the proposal's own conditional phrasing is honest about this. It's integrity hygiene on a non-production, mock-graded evaluator, not a live-decision fix.

Grounding is mixed. The honest-verification leg ("separating generation from evaluation is the strongest lever") is real Anthropic guidance and is cited in the code itself (evaluator_agent.py:5) -- it fully supports the highest-value action (delete the fabricated stub), and the proposal does not depend on the other citation for that. The structured-outputs leg cites a REAL Anthropic feature but MISAPPLIES it: _call_model uses the google-genai client, so Anthropic's output_config.format does not exist on that path -- the correct mechanism is Gemini's response_schema/response_mime_type. Real doc, wrong vendor for this code: an imprecision, not a fabrication, so not a REJECT -- but the implementer must use the Gemini mechanism, and adding schema enforcement to a non-prod mock-graded path is gold-plating (the most effort for the least value here).

Recommendation if codified: trim to the unambiguous, safe core -- delete _run_spot_checks + evaluate_with_spot_checks (removing fabricated evaluator evidence) and fix the stale docstring; WARN-on-mock is a cheap, sensible add. Make the structured-output change optional and, if done, implement it via Gemini response_schema, NOT Anthropic output_config.format. Worth doing when convenient; not a priority step ahead of live-money or live-harness work.

rejected.title: /goal wrapping for cycle-2 retry (unattended CONDITIONAL auto-fix)
rejected.current_state: Cycle-2 (fix CONDITIONAL blockers + re-spawn Q/A) is manual today. Main writes violation_details to handoff; operator reads; manually re-runs `/masterplan fix` or re-prompts. Takes 1-2 operator interactions per CONDITIONAL. Run-harness.py has no loop-until-PASS; exits on first Q/A verdict.
rejected.proposed_change: Wrap the phase-generate-evaluate block in `/goal "Q/A verdict is PASS, or stop after 10 turns"`. When Q/A returns CONDITIONAL, /goal evaluator detects the non-PASS state and keeps the session running. Main auto-reads handoff/current/evaluator_critique.json (via Workflow structured output from proposal 2), extracts violated_criteria, writes fix prompt + updated files, re-spawns Q/A via Workflow. Repeat until PASS or turn budget exhausted. Evaluator model (Haiku) checks one condition; fast. Operator sets the turn budget when starting the harness; unattended otherwise.
rejected.grounding: Anthropic Claude Code /goal feature (code.claude.com/docs/en/goal.md): 'After each turn, a small fast model checks whether the condition holds. If not, Claude starts another turn instead of returning control to you.' Designed for iterative work with a measurable end state. /goal is compatible with subagent spawning and file I/O. Harness already uses file-based communication per Anthropic's documented pattern ('Communication was handled via files: one agent would write a file, another agent would read it'); /goal orchestrates that file-based loop.
rejected.expected_benefit: Unattended cycle-2: 80% of CONDITIONALs (simple blocker fixes) resolve without operator re-trigger. Reduces manual harness babysitting. Operator still has a turn budget (10-turn cap prevents runaway loops). Leaves the file-based fresh-respawn pattern (Workflow script reads updated handoff files; cycle-2 is a NEW file state, not second-opinion-shopping on old evidence). Aligns with Anthropic's harness design (goal-based loops are the recommended pattern for 'keep working until condition X').
rejected.risk: RUNAWAY: if the blocker is a logic flaw Main can't fix (e.g., Q/A rejects a contract premise), /goal burns all 10 turns fixing the unfixable. Mitigation: Main inspects violated_criteria and decides if the fix is plausible (checks for 'contract premise' keywords in violation text) before re-looping. Second risk: /goal evaluator (Haiku) misunderstands the PASS check. Mitigation: explicit condition phrasing ('Q/A writes PASS in the structured verdict JSON file'). Also, /goal runs on the main session's model (Opus) per turn, so thinking still happens; just the meta-check is Haiku.
rejected.fits_constraints: Respects 3-agent: /goal is a harness-level feature, not a 4th agent. Researcher + Q/A unchanged. Preserves file-based handoffs (Main writes updated results.md, Q/A reads it). Separation of duties: Q/A evaluator still independent; /goal is just the retry orchestrator. Max-plan: /goal is stdlib, zero cost. Turn budget is operator-defined, so cost-control is explicit.

verdict.reasoning:
Grounding VERIFIED REAL (not a fake-citation reject): `/goal` exists (code.claude.com/docs/en/goal.md), requires v2.1.139+ (we run 2.1.211), and the cited quote is verbatim-accurate. It is NOT a 4th L3 agent — the doc calls it "a wrapper around a session-scoped prompt-based Stop hook," i.e. harness plumbing; on Max the Haiku evaluator is flat-fee/negligible, so $0-metered + local-only hold. REJECT is on merit, not grounding.

Why REJECT: this points a real feature at the single most safety-critical, load-bearing gate in the harness for a marginal payoff.
1) Erodes evaluator independence (a BINDING constraint). A loop whose terminal condition is literally "reach Q/A PASS" manufactures structural pressure toward superficial verdict-flipping — the exact anti-pattern in auto-memory feedback_harness_rigor ("stop rigging dual-evaluator PASS"). Q/A stays a fresh subagent, so it's not an outright violation, but it corrodes the property the rule exists to protect.
2) Contradicts failure-discipline. Condition "PASS or stop after 10 turns" has NO FAIL exit and ignores the 3rd-CONDITIONAL->auto-FAIL rule; FAIL means revert/certified_fallback, not "keep fixing to green."
3) Unattended-escalation risk. The PostToolUse auto-commit-and-push hook fires on masterplan status->done; an unattended run-to-completion risks an unreviewed push to origin/main — the precise pattern the operator was burned by and reverted (feedback_background_agent_resumption_risk).
4) Inoperable as written: doc says the Haiku evaluator "doesn't run commands or read files independently," so keying the condition to "Q/A writes PASS in the structured verdict JSON FILE" cannot work — it can only judge the transcript.
5) Category confusion: run_harness.py is the param-optimization loop (heuristic planner -> QuantOptimizer -> backtest evaluator); it never spawns the Q/A subagent — that's the Main-session masterplan cycle — so "run_harness.py exits on first Q/A verdict" mis-describes the target.
6) Marginal benefit vs M-effort + maintenance on the harness's most sensitive gate; saves 1-2 operator touches per CONDITIONAL against an operator whose revealed preference is MORE review, not less.

Constructive note (not grounds to keep this version): the safe use of `/goal` would target a DETERMINISTIC pre-Q/A GENERATE end-state (e.g. "verification command exits 0 and syntax checks pass"), matching Anthropic's canonical examples (tests pass / build compiles) — never the Q/A judgment gate. The genuinely high-leverage sibling here is codifying the Workflow-structured-output Q/A path (feedback_workflow_qa_when_subagents_stall) into run_harness.py; that is a different, lower-risk proposal this one merely rides on.

rejected.title: Hooks with effort.level gating for conditional verification (cost control per phase tier)
rejected.current_state: PostToolUse hooks (archive-handoff, auto-commit-and-push, live_check_gate) run unconditionally on every Write/Edit. No awareness of harness effort level. Live_check_gate hook spawns archive verification even when effort='low' (e.g., routine bugfixes). Cost is low but batch cost across 10 steps adds up. No mechanism to skip expensive verification on routine work.
rejected.proposed_change: Hooks read $CLAUDE_EFFORT env var (available since v2.1.141). Add conditional logic to archive-handoff and live_check_gate hooks: if effort.level == 'low', skip expensive file-format validation + pre-commit linting (keep lightweight checks like file-exists). If effort.level >= 'high', run full validation. live_check_gate specifically: only prompt for live-system evidence (curl, BQ query, screenshot) if effort >= 'xhigh'; on 'high' / 'medium', do lightweight file-existence check only. StatusMessage in settings.json documents the logic for future readers.
rejected.grounding: Claude Code hooks reference (code.claude.com/docs/en/hooks-guide.md): 'Hooks receive the active effort tier via the effort.level JSON input field AND the $CLAUDE_EFFORT env var.' (v2.1.141+). Pattern documented: gate expensive operations on effort level. Pyfinagent example: Q/A subagent maxTurns=30 (high effort) vs. research brief could run at lower turns if effort were low. Same principle applies to hook verification: full linting when xhigh, lightweight when low.
rejected.expected_benefit: Cost control on per-phase basis: small phases (e.g., minor doc updates) set effort='low', skip expensive linting. Large risky phases keep effort='xhigh', run full checks. Batch cost savings: ~10% reduction on PostToolUse hook overhead on routine cycles. Operator control via phase-level effort setting in masterplan.json (proposal for new field: phase-level effort override). Aligns with Anthropic's effort-level guidance: lower effort for routine work, full thinking for high-stakes decisions.
rejected.risk: Silent skips: if effort logic is wrong, operator doesn't know verification was skipped. Mitigation: always log which checks were run vs. skipped to handoff/logs/. Verification consistency: if a step that should have been verified (effort='low' but should've been 'high') ships a bug, the gap was masked by effort gating. Mitigation: operator reviews effort assignments in masterplan.json before running; QA step 1 checks effort level. Very low risk if logging is comprehensive.
rejected.fits_constraints: Purely mechanical hook change; no agent re-split. File-based handoffs unchanged (hooks still write the same files, just fewer checks on them). No impact on harness protocol or separation of duties. Max-plan: effort level is already computed; this is just reading an env var (zero cost).

verdict.reasoning:
REJECT. The grounding feature is real ($CLAUDE_EFFORT / effort.level, Claude Code v2.1.141+, confirmed in CLAUDE.md phase-40.2), but its application here is a misapplication resting on a factually false premise, and it actively weakens a load-bearing constraint. Verified against the actual code:

(1) PREMISE FALSE — the "expensive file-format validation + pre-commit linting" this proposal wants to skip does not exist in the target hooks. I read both: live_check_gate.py is a pure masterplan JSON-read + Path.exists() (microseconds) and is ALREADY conditional (only fires when the operator set verification.live_check on a step); archive-handoff.sh just walks the plan and cp/git-mv's handoff files. grep across all of .claude/hooks/ for flake8|black|eslint|mypy|ruff|ast.parse|jsonschema|lint returned nothing. The only pre-commit validation (pre-commit-env-check.sh) is .env-scoped, zero-cost when none staged, is a git pre-commit hook (not a PostToolUse hook the proposal names), and is still local/$0. There is nothing expensive to gate.

(2) "COST CONTROL" VOID under the $0-metered binding constraint — every one of these hooks is local bash/git/Python with no LLM call, so it already costs $0 metered. "~10% reduction on hook overhead" is 10% of a few local-CPU seconds on Peder's Mac, explicitly not a token saving. Anthropic's effort-cost guidance governs model token spend, not deterministic shell hooks — category error.

(3) ENABLING MECHANISM DOESN'T EXIST — effort in pyfinagent is a session-level constant (settings.json effortLevel=xhigh), not per-phase. grep confirms masterplan.json has no effort field; the proposal admits it is inventing one ("proposal for new field"). So the low-effort→skip branch would essentially never fire for Main (always xhigh); the proposal bundles a speculative schema change to make its own premise work. $CLAUDE_EFFORT is referenced nowhere in the repo yet.

(4) WEAKENS THE VERIFICATION BACKBONE — the live_check gate exists precisely to convert "agent claimed PASS" into an operator-auditable artifact and to attack the VERIFICATION_DEFECT pattern (dev-MAS audit R-1). Gating operator-requested live evidence on the model's thinking budget is backwards; verification rigor must be independent of reasoning effort. This contradicts auto-memory feedback_harness_rigor, feedback_qa_harness_compliance_first, and the phase-69 audit burn-down. The proposal's own risk section concedes silent skips could mask shipped bugs, mitigated only by adding logging complexity — net-negative value. Cargo-cult complexity; multiple independent grounds for rejection.

rejected.title: Worktrees with subagent isolation for Q/A (parallel file I/O safety)
rejected.current_state: All agents (Main + Researcher + Q/A) operate in the same git checkout. When Q/A reads handoff files while Main writes them in a hook, file-write ordering is not guaranteed. Unlikely but possible: Q/A reads a half-written .json file during hook execution. Observed: no actual file-corruption issues, but the risk exists. Also: Q/A Bash calls (test -f, grep, python -c) run in the same directory as Main's edits, so symlink/permission changes from Main could affect Q/A mid-evaluation.
rejected.proposed_change: Configure Researcher and Q/A subagents with `isolation: worktree` in their .md frontmatter. Each subagent gets a fresh worktree with its own copy of the handoff files at spawn time. Workflow or Agent tool passes the worktree path to the subagent. Subagent reads/inspects handoff files from its isolated checkout; zero write-ordering concerns. Main's hooks continue to modify the primary checkout; worktree is read-only to subagent. On subagent finish, the worktree is auto-cleaned (no uncommitted changes, fresh at each spawn).
rejected.grounding: Claude Code worktrees doc (code.claude.com/docs/en/worktrees.md): 'Subagents can run in their own worktrees so parallel edits don't conflict.' Frontmatter field `isolation: worktree` is officially supported. 'Each subagent gets a temporary worktree that is removed automatically when the subagent finishes without changes.' Pattern: 'Subagent worktrees use the same base branch as --worktree, so they branch from your repository's default branch unless worktree.baseRef is set to "head".' Pyfinagent already pins worktree.baseRef in its git config; worktree overhead is well-documented.
rejected.expected_benefit: File I/O safety: Q/A reads a consistent snapshot of handoff files; no race conditions. Simplifies lock-free verification logic (Q/A doesn't need to retry-on-stale-read). Small operational benefit: if Main crashes mid-commit, subagent's worktree remains clean and doesn't inherit the broken state. Documentation value: isolation: worktree makes the 3-agent model's boundaries explicit (each agent has its own filesystem scope).
rejected.risk: Git worktree overhead: each subagent spawn costs ~200-500ms for `git worktree add`. With Researcher + Q/A per step, that's ~1-2 seconds extra per cycle. Measurable but acceptable (harness cycles already take minutes). Cleanup risk: if a hook crashes while a worktree exists, the worktree isn't auto-removed (requires manual `git worktree remove`). Mitigation: add a cleanup hook that runs on session exit. Very low risk if cleanup is automated.
rejected.fits_constraints: Orthogonal to agent-tool vs. Workflow question (either driver works with worktree isolation). No harness protocol changes. Respects 3-agent rule (Researcher + Q/A just get more isolated copies). File-based handoffs still the same; just safer. Max-plan: git overhead is local machine (zero API cost).

verdict.reasoning:
REJECT. The cited feature IS real (isolation: worktree is a genuine Claude Code subagent frontmatter field — confirmed in Claude Code sub-agents docs, Boris Cherny's built-in-worktree announcement, and GitHub issues #27023/#50357), so I reject on merit, not non-existence. Three decisive failures:

1) SOLVES A NON-PROBLEM. The feature's documented purpose is preventing parallel EDITS from conflicting. pyfinagent's harness has none. Subagents are single-turn synchronous and the protocol is strictly sequential: Main writes handoff files during GENERATE, THEN spawns Q/A during EVALUATE while Main is blocked awaiting the return — no concurrent writes. Q/A is read-only (tools: Read/Bash/Glob/Grep, verified in qa.md); Researcher writes only its own brief. There are no parallel edits to the handoff files to isolate. The proposal itself admits "no actual file-corruption issues." The 'half-written .json during hook execution' race window is structurally ~nil.

2) IT WOULD ACTIVELY BREAK THE LOAD-BEARING FILE HANDOFF (fatal). Verified via git log + .claude/hooks/auto-commit-and-push.sh + protocol: the rolling handoff files (experiment_results.md, evaluator_critique.md) are committed ONLY by the status-flip auto-commit hook, which runs AFTER Q/A passes. So at Q/A-evaluation time they are UNCOMMITTED working-tree state. `git worktree add` checks out COMMITTED state (HEAD/base branch) and does NOT carry uncommitted working-tree changes into the new worktree. A worktree-isolated Q/A would therefore read the PREVIOUS cycle's stale experiment_results.md, never the fresh evidence Main just wrote — a silent correctness regression in the single most load-bearing gate in the whole harness. The cycle-2 'fresh Q/A on UPDATED evidence' flow breaks identically (fix is uncommitted, worktree can't see it). baseRef:head does not help — HEAD is the last commit, not the working tree. The proposal frames worktree/primary-checkout decoupling as a safety feature; in this architecture it is exactly the bug, because Q/A's evidence lives in the uncommitted working tree.

3) CONFABULATED GROUNDING + COLLIDES WITH THE REAL FIX. The grounding claim 'Pyfinagent already pins worktree.baseRef in its git config' is FALSE — `git config --get-regexp worktree` returns nothing; there are zero worktree keys. And the known subagent-stall pain already has a codified mitigation (run Q/A via Workflow structured-output, feedback_workflow_qa_when_subagents_stall); isolation frontmatter is only honored on certain spawn paths (#50357), so it fights that path. Net: adds git-worktree overhead + orphaned-worktree cleanup surface on a local-only single-Mac deployment for a benefit that is at best zero and at worst a stale-evidence correctness bug in the evaluator gate. Not a hard constraint violation (git is local $0; 3-agent rule intact), but a clear cost/benefit and correctness reject.

rejected.title: Fallback model auto-recovery on subagent stalls (Opus → Sonnet automatic downgrade)
rejected.current_state: .claude/settings.json has fallbackModel chain: ["claude-opus-4-8", "claude-sonnet-5"]. Feature exists but untested. When Q/A stalls, operator manually edits qa.md to downgrade model. No automatic fallback in the harness loop itself. CLAUDE.md notes: 'Stall watch: two Fable Q/A spawns stalled mid-evaluation 2026-07-09 (Cycle-76 addendum) -- if a fresh-session Fable Q/A stalls, revert the qa.md pin immediately; Opus is the reliable evaluator.'
rejected.proposed_change: Add fallback logic to run_harness.py: track consecutive_Q/A_stalls counter. When Q/A Workflow/Agent returns a timeout or hanging-turn-limit error (detected via Workflow status or Agent-tool timeout), increment counter. At counter >= 2 consecutive stalls, Main auto-downgrades Q/A subagent model via a temp .claude/agents/qa-fallback.md (or inline model override in the Workflow script). Spawn Q/A again with `model: claude-sonnet-5` (fallback). If the fallback Q/A completes, log the downgrade to harness_log.md + reset counter. If fallback also stalls, escalate to certified_fallback (known-good old parameters). On next cycle, check if Opus is healthy again (counter reset after 24h or operator re-approval).
rejected.grounding: Anthropic Claude Code fallbackModel doc (code.claude.com/docs/en/model-config.md): 'Fallback model chain in settings.json allows automatic downgrade on failure.' Pyfinagent settings already has the chain defined. Anthropic's 'Building Effective Agents' recommends cost-adaptive fallback for gate roles. Pattern: metered roles (per-ticker Layer-2 agents) don't fallback (cost control), but rare-event roles (Layer-3) can fallback because frequency is low.
rejected.expected_benefit: Auto-recovery from model-specific stalls without operator intervention. If an Opus overload surge causes mid-evaluation timeouts, Sonnet fallback completes the cycle and operator only needs to re-approve Opus for the next day. Aligns with known issue (CLAUDE.md): 'Fable Q/A spawns stalled mid-evaluation 2026-07-09 -- if a fresh-session Fable Q/A stalls again, revert the qa.md pin immediately.' Automatic revert would avoid manual step. Cost is bounded: fallback only triggers on stall (not every cycle), and Sonnet pricing is 1/10th of Opus.
rejected.risk: Model quality variance: Sonnet evaluator may miss nuanced contract violations that Opus catches. Mitigation: when Sonnet returns PASS, require stricter re-check on next cycle (higher linting bar) to compensate for lower evaluator depth. Stall detection timing: how long is 'too long' for Q/A to hang? Current timeout is unclear (Agent-tool has no explicit timeout; just hangs). Mitigation: hardcode a 5-minute Workflow timeout, capture as ERROR status, trigger fallback. Rare but possible: both Opus and Sonnet stall on the same critique due to a hard blocker (not model issue). Mitigation: certified_fallback escalation after fallback also stalls, same as today.
rejected.fits_constraints: Purely operational tuning; no harness-protocol changes. Respects 3-agent rule (only tweaks Q/A model pin). Max-plan: fallback cost is contained (rare-event phase, only on stall). Separation of duties: Q/A is still independent; Main just chooses the model. File-based handoffs unchanged.

verdict.reasoning: REJECT. Both grounding claims fail verification, and the core mechanism targets the wrong root cause. GROUNDING: (1) The official Claude Code model-config doc ("Fallback model chains") states the native fallbackModel fires ONLY when "the primary model is overloaded, unavailable, or returns another non-retryable server error" and that it is already automatic and per-turn. A mid-turn end-flush STALL is not a non-retryable server error, so the native feature does not cover the proposal's target case; and for the overload case it DOES cover, Claude Code already downgrades automatically. The project already adopted this exact chain in phase-67.5 (["claude-opus-4-8","claude-sonnet-5"], Q/A PASS), and settings.json's own statusMessage already documents "covers OVERLOAD-class failures only." (2) I fetched "Building Effective Agents": it does NOT recommend cost-adaptive fallback or failure-triggered model downgrading for gate roles — only static routing (easy->Haiku, hard->Sonnet). That grounding pillar is fabricated. FATAL SUBSTANCE: (a) Wrong root cause — the project's own auto-memory feedback_workflow_qa_when_subagents_stall documents the Agent-tool stall as "intermittent and NOT model-specific — Opus stalled as often as Fable"; a model swap cannot fix a model-agnostic hang, and Sonnet would stall on end-flush too. The real, already-discovered fix is the Workflow structured-output Q/A path (verdict = captured return value, immune to file-write stall, with runtime retry) — a separate, better upgrade this proposal ignores. (b) Architecturally misplaced — run_harness.py is deterministic and per its own docstring "does not invoke Claude directly"; its run_evaluator() is in-process statistical validation, NOT the Q/A LLM subagent, which Main spawns in the session layer. So run_harness.py never observes a Q/A subagent stall and cannot host the proposed counter as described. (c) Inoperable primary mechanism — the "temp qa-fallback.md mid-session" path violates the roster-snapshot rule (CLAUDE.md: agent .md files snapshot at session start; mid-session adds aren't dispatchable until restart); the "inline Workflow model override" alternative is unverified hand-waving. (d) Doctrine conflict — CLAUDE.md: "Opus is the reliable evaluator"; the documented remedy is revert-TO-Opus, not auto-downgrade to a weaker Sonnet gate, which risks rubber-stamping against feedback_harness_rigor ("qa must push back"), and the proposal's own "stricter re-check next cycle" mitigation is vague added machinery to paper over a self-inflicted evaluator-quality regression. NET: duplicates a native, already-adopted feature for the one case a swap helps; misapplies a model swap to a model-agnostic stall it cannot fix; not implementable where proposed. Priority is moot on a REJECT (P3 nominal).

rejected.title: MCP alwaysLoad tuning for Q/A evaluation phase (defer expensive tool servers)
rejected.current_state: .mcp.json pinning: pyfinagent-data (alwaysLoad: true), pyfinagent-risk (alwaysLoad: true), pyfinagent-backtest/signals (alwaysLoad: false). Q/A subagent can call all tools including multi-thousand-line signal servers. On Q/A spawn, Claude Code loads *all* configured servers, adding ~2-5s startup latency. Only ~10% of Q/A evals actually call backtest or signals tools (mostly BQ inspection + Bash checks).
rejected.proposed_change: Create a `.mcp-qa.json` overlay (or Q/A-specific MCP config) that sets pyfinagent-signals, pyfinagent-backtest to alwaysLoad: false (defer to ToolSearch on first reference). Keep pyfinagent-data alwaysLoad: true (BQ inspection is common). Q/A frontmatter points to this config via a future `mcpConfig` field (if supported by Claude Code) or Main manually sets CLAUDE_MCP_CONFIG_DIR env var before spawning Q/A subagent. Result: Q/A startup is ~1-2s faster; tools load on-demand if needed.
rejected.grounding: Claude Code .mcp.json phase-40.2 adoption (CLAUDE.md): 'alwaysLoad: true|false controls whether that server's tools skip tool-search deferral at session start.' Pattern: rarely-used tools (signals) defer; always-used tools (data inspection) load upfront. Pyfinagent already implements this for Layer-3 Main session; proposal extends to subagent scope. Per-agent MCP config is not yet a first-class subagent frontmatter field, so fallback is ENV var override at spawn time (run_harness.py can set CLAUDE_MCP_CONFIG_DIR before Workflow/Agent-tool call).
rejected.expected_benefit: Q/A spawn latency: ~2-3s savings per cycle = ~20-30s per 10-step masterplan (micro-optimization). Psychological: faster spawn feels more responsive. No functional benefit (Q/A can still call signals/backtest via ToolSearch). Operational: reduces initial session-startup cost for experimenting with Q/A changes.
rejected.risk: Fragility: if Q/A can't find a tool because ToolSearch isn't triggered, prompt fails. Mitigation: test Q/A with the override config on a CONDITIONAL-verdict step to verify signals/backtest tools are accessible on-demand. Per-agent MCP config not yet a standard feature (workaround is ENV var), so future Claude Code upgrades may require re-implementation. Low risk if testing is comprehensive.
rejected.fits_constraints: Purely configuration-level tuning; no harness-protocol changes. Max-plan: faster startup is local savings (zero API cost). Orthogonal to agent split / file-based handoffs. Respects separation of duties.

verdict.reasoning:
REJECT on four independent grounds, any one sufficient.

(1) ZERO ACTUAL DELTA — dispositive. Verified against /Users/ford/.openclaw/workspace/pyfinagent/.mcp.json: pyfinagent-backtest is ALREADY alwaysLoad:false (line 44) and pyfinagent-signals is ALREADY alwaysLoad:false (line 77). The proposed "Q/A overlay" sets exactly those two to false and keeps pyfinagent-data true (already true, line 55) — every server value it names is IDENTICAL to the current global config. Net change to what loads on a Q/A spawn: nothing. The premise "On Q/A spawn, Claude Code loads *all* configured servers, adding ~2-5s startup latency" is factually false and self-contradictory: it contradicts the alwaysLoad:false flags the proposal's own current_state correctly lists. The only alwaysLoad:true servers are data + risk, both of which the proposal keeps loaded (and risk = kill-switch/PBO gates is genuinely relevant to evaluating trading-code, so deferring it would be wrong anyway).

(2) GROUNDING MECHANISM DOES NOT EXIST. The cited feature (phase-40.2 alwaysLoad, quoted correctly from CLAUDE.md) is real but misapplied. The delivery mechanism is not: per-subagent MCP config via frontmatter (mcpConfig) is an OPEN feature request (GitHub anthropics/claude-code#51274), not shipped — the proposal itself admits "not yet a first-class subagent frontmatter field." The CLAUDE_MCP_CONFIG_DIR env-var fallback is undocumented/unverified; the nearest real thing, CLAUDE_CONFIG_DIR launcher scripts, is "heavyweight" and explicitly "doesn't help sub-agents within a session." Task rule: if the cited mechanism doesn't exist, REJECT.

(3) SELF-ADMITTED NON-BENEFIT / CARGO-CULT. The proposal states "No functional benefit," "Psychological: faster spawn feels more responsive," "micro-optimization (~2-3s/cycle)," confidence "low." Adding a second config file for an aesthetic/psychological effect is precisely the cargo-cult complexity to reject. Benefit is marginal-to-nil; cost is a permanent .mcp-qa.json that must be kept in lockstep with .mcp.json, plus fragility (tool-not-found → prompt fails).

(4) OPTIMIZES A DEPRECATED PATH. The known pain point (auto-memory feedback_workflow_qa_when_subagents_stall) moves Q/A onto the Workflow-tool structured-output path — the very path this evaluation runs on — precisely because Agent-tool subagent spawns intermittently stall. Tuning Agent-tool Q/A spawn latency polishes a mechanism the project is actively migrating away from.

No hard binding-constraint violation (it doesn't touch the 3-agent count, self-eval ban, or file-handoff backbone), but it fails the real/grounded/high-leverage/worth-the-cost bar on every axis. P3 is nominal — leverage is effectively zero.

rejected.title: Hooks with continueOnBlock for live_check retry (re-prompt on evidence failure)
rejected.current_state: live_check_gate hook (phase-23.8.1) checks for handoff/current/live_check_<step_id>.md. If missing, hook logs WARN and skips auto-push. No retry mechanism; operator must manually create the file and re-trigger. Hook type is command-type, which doesn't support continueOnBlock. When the hook blocks, the entire PostToolUse phase halts (auto-commit doesn't happen).
rejected.proposed_change: Convert live_check_gate to a prompt-type hook (requires Claude Code v2.1.139+) with continueOnBlock: true. Prompt checks for live_check_*.md file; if missing, prompt sends feedback to Claude: 'Live check evidence is missing for step <id>. Examples: curl output showing /api/endpoint response, BQ query showing row in paper_trades table, screenshot path of UI state. Add evidence to handoff/current/live_check_<step_id>.md in Markdown format with command + output.' If operator sees the prompt, they can provide evidence inline; hook continues (auto-commit proceeds, live_check_gate logs CONTINUE status). If operator doesn't respond within 2 turns, hook auto-blocks as before (skip push, log to auto-push.log). Separates the gate (does evidence exist?) from the orchestration (commit/push).
rejected.grounding: Claude Code hooks v2.1.139+ (code.claude.com/docs/en/hooks-guide.md): 'Hook type can be prompt-type with continueOnBlock: true. When set, it feeds the hook's rejection reason back to Claude and continues the turn instead of halting.' Pyfinagent's current live_check_gate is a Bash command-type hook; conversion to prompt-type is straightforward (shell dispatch to a prompt string). Documented use case: 'verify a blocking condition before allowing an action; if blocked, give Claude a chance to remediate and re-check.'
rejected.expected_benefit: Reduces operator friction on live_check gate: if evidence is missing and operator is watching the session, they can add it immediately without re-running the masterplan step. Keeps the gate verifiable (evidence must be provided; no auto-PASS rubber-stamp) but makes it interactive. Audit trail: continueOnBlock prompt is captured in the transcript, so auditors can see what evidence was requested vs. provided. Aligns with phase-23.8.1 design intent: 'convert "the agent claimed PASS" into "an artifact exists that an operator can audit".'
rejected.risk: Blocking semantics change: hook no longer hard-blocks on missing evidence; it prompts instead. If operator doesn't respond, push is skipped, which matches current behavior, but the operator might not realize evidence is missing (prompt feedback might be overlooked). Mitigation: auto-push.log.WARN is still logged; operator sees it on next masterplan check. Also: prompts in hooks add a turn, so if the operator is away, the session waits for 10+ minutes for the prompt timeout. Mitigation: timeout is configurable (set to 2 turns / 5 min); hook then auto-blocks as before. Low risk if timeouts are reasonable.
rejected.fits_constraints: Purely hook-level enhancement; no harness-protocol changes. File-based handoffs unchanged (live_check_*.md file is still the artifact). Max-plan: prompt is on the main session (already on flat-fee). Respects separation of duties (hook is just a gate, not an evaluator).

verdict.reasoning:
REJECT. The grounding is real (continueOnBlock is documented in CLAUDE.md phase-40.2, Claude Code v2.1.139+), but it is misapplied to the wrong hook, and the premise is factually wrong.

(1) Misrepresents current state. The proposal claims the current command-hook "halts the entire PostToolUse phase (auto-commit doesn't happen)." The actual code (.claude/hooks/auto-commit-and-push.sh + lib/live_check_gate.py) proves the opposite: on a missing artifact the gate does `log "WARN..."; exit 0` — a graceful, fail-OPEN, non-blocking skip that explicitly "never blocks the masterplan Write." The helper "NEVER raises -- fail-open to 'proceed'." There is no hard halt to fix; the problem being solved does not exist.

(2) Weakens a load-bearing verification gate (constraint conflict). continueOnBlock "feeds the hook's rejection reason back to Claude" — i.e. to Main, the orchestrator being gated. The live_check gate (phase-23.8.1 / audit R-1) exists specifically to attack the VERIFICATION_DEFECT pattern: convert "the agent claimed PASS" into "an artifact an operator can audit," requiring operator-gathered LIVE-system evidence (curl/BQ/screenshot). Re-prompting Main to write its own live_check_*.md file lets the gated agent self-clear its own audit gate — a rubber-stamp / self-eval risk that undercuts the gate's entire purpose and brushes the self-evaluation-forbidden constraint. In unattended/away-ops runs (phases 62-65) no operator is watching, so Main auto-satisfies the gate itself. CLAUDE.md's own sanctioned continueOnBlock use is an ORCHESTRATION retry (auto-commit-stall mitigation), not self-producing VERIFICATION evidence — right feature, wrong hook.

(3) Autonomy/stall regression. It converts a fail-open exit-0 skip into a turn-adding prompt that can wait for a response; the proposal itself admits a 10+ min stall when the operator is away. This fights the away-ops program AND the active anti-stall direction (the 55s cap was retired in phase-67; subagent end-flush stalls are a live known pain per feedback_workflow_qa_when_subagents_stall). The current WARN->exit-0->proceed behavior is strictly better for unattended autonomy.

(4) Marginal benefit. A watching operator already has a trivial documented path (create the file + no-op masterplan edit, or `git push origin main`); the saved re-trigger step is minor. Self-rated confidence medium.

rejected.title: Generator self-review punch-list before the Q/A spawn (evaluator-optimizer, generator side)
rejected.current_state: The GENERATE phase (docs/runbooks/per-step-protocol.md §3, lines 99-109) says only 'Do the work. Run the verification command.' Main hands straight to Q/A with no structured self-review. The only self-discipline codified is the ban on self-EVALUATION (Main reporting PASS without Q/A). There is no pre-handoff generation-quality pass, so obvious defects (unmet contract deliverables, un-run mutation test, missing smoke) reach the independent Q/A spawn — which is both the most expensive and the known stall point (Agent-tool Q/A stalled 6x on 2026-07-11).
rejected.proposed_change: Add a mandatory 'Pre-Q/A self-review' step to GENERATE. Before spawning Q/A, Main appends a short punch-list to experiment_results.md that (a) enumerates each contract deliverable and marks present/absent, (b) records that Main ran the mutation-resistance test itself (inject planted violation → confirm detection → restore), (c) lists known gaps/scope bounds. This is explicitly a generation-QUALITY checklist, NOT a verdict — the binding PASS still comes only from the independent Q/A. Add one line to qa.md: Q/A confirms the punch-list exists and cross-checks it (a generator that skipped self-review is a scope-honesty smell).
rejected.grounding: Anthropic 'Harness design for long-running apps' — the generator does 'self-evaluation [of] its work at the end of each sprint before handing off to QA' (https://www.anthropic.com/engineering/harness-design-long-running-apps). This is the generator-side half of the evaluator-optimizer loop in 'Building Effective Agents' (https://www.anthropic.com/engineering/building-effective-agents), applied WITHOUT displacing the independent evaluator.
rejected.expected_benefit: Cheaper, faster cycles — defects caught before the expensive, stall-prone Q/A spawn; fewer FAIL→fix→re-Q/A loops; a durable pre-handoff artifact that makes scope honesty auditable. Directly shrinks exposure to the 6x-stall failure mode by reducing the number of Q/A round-trips per step.
rejected.risk: If worded loosely, Main could treat the punch-list AS the verdict and skip Q/A. Mitigation: the runbook must state 'punch-list ≠ verdict; PASS requires the independent Q/A,' and Q/A's LLM-judgment leg checks that the punch-list is present but does not treat it as authoritative.
rejected.fits_constraints: Respects 'self-evaluation by Main is forbidden' — the punch-list is a checklist, not a verdict; PASS still requires the independent Q/A. No new agent (stays exactly-3 at L3). Keeps the 5-file protocol (extends experiment_results.md). Max-plan flat-fee.

verdict.reasoning:
REJECT — the grounding is misrepresented (inverted), which is a hard-reject trigger, and the benefit is marginal/redundant with drift risk against the harness's most load-bearing constraint.

GROUNDING CHECK (decisive): The cited quote IS real in Anthropic's "Harness design for long-running apps" — "the generator was instructed to self-evaluate its work at the end of each sprint before handing off to QA." But I fetched the full context: it sits in the "Why naive implementations fall short" section as the FLAWED BASELINE, not a recommendation. It is immediately followed by "agents tend to respond by confidently praising the work—even when the quality is obviously mediocre" and "identify legitimate issues, then talk themselves into approving the work anyway." The article's conclusion is the OPPOSITE of the proposal: "Separating the agent doing the work from the agent judging it proves to be a strong lever." The article contains NO recommendation for a generator self-review punch-list before handoff. This exact passage is the ORIGIN of pyfinagent's own "self-evaluation by Main is forbidden" rule (CLAUDE.md's "agents tend to confidently praise their own work" is lifted verbatim from it). The proposal would re-import the precise anti-pattern the project correctly banned. The secondary grounding ("Building Effective Agents" evaluator-optimizer) is also misapplied — that pattern is explicitly "one LLM generates... another provides evaluation," a SEPARATE evaluator, never generator self-review. Per instructions, a cited doc that "does not say what is claimed" = REJECT.

REDUNDANT: All three punch-list items already live in Q/A's independent LLM-judgment leg (.claude/agents/qa.md lines 197-205): contract/deliverable alignment, the mutation-resistance test (inject planted violation → confirm detection → restore), and scope-honesty disclosure. The proposal adds a self-ATTESTED layer on top of checks already done more reliably by the separated evaluator.

DRIFT RISK vs LOAD-BEARING CONSTRAINT: A checklist where Main marks its own deliverables "present" is exactly the low-signal generator self-assessment the source calls unreliable. The proposal's own stated risk (Main treats the punch-list as the verdict, under-weights Q/A) is mitigated only by "word the runbook carefully" — a known-weak control given documented drift (research gate skipped 7/9 phase-4.8 cycles despite "MUST BE USED"; Main self-evaluates under time pressure). It manufactures a self-eval-adjacent ritual against the project's #1 drift mode and the single most load-bearing constraint (Q/A independence / no Main self-eval).

WEAK LEVERAGE ON THE REAL PAIN: The claimed benefit (shrink the 6x-stall exposure by reducing Q/A round-trips) is speculative and indirect. The stall failure mode already has a direct, dedicated fix identified — the Workflow-tool structured-output Q/A path (feedback_workflow_qa_when_subagents_stall) — which is the actual high-leverage move, not this.

Constraints otherwise fine (no new agent, $0 metered, local-only), but a misgrounded, redundant change that erodes the self-eval ban for marginal gain does not clear the bar. Priority P3 only because the schema requires one; this is a reject, not a nice-to-have.

rejected.title: Make parallel researcher fan-out the default for complex/deep gates (orchestrator-worker parallelization)
rejected.current_state: researcher.md contemplates a 2-3 instance fork ONLY at the deep tier and only opt-in (lines 242-257). The complex tier runs a single serial researcher session even when the step has 3+ separable sub-questions, forgoing the isolated-context parallelization the multi-agent doc reports as the largest research-latency lever.
rejected.proposed_change: When the coverage ledger (prior proposal) shows >=3 separable sub-questions, Main fans out parallel researcher INSTANCES (same role, isolated contexts) partitioned by sub-question, then merges the read-in-full tables (dedup URLs, label by instance origin). Codify the partition key = the coverage ledger, and keep the per-instance source floor. This promotes the existing opt-in deep-tier fork to a default for qualifying complex/deep steps.
rejected.grounding: Anthropic 'How we built our multi-agent research system' — the lead 'spawns 3-5 subagents in parallel rather than serially,' cutting research time 'by up to 90% for complex queries'; 'complex research might use more than 10 subagents'; separation of concerns via isolated contexts (https://www.anthropic.com/engineering/multi-agent-research-system).
rejected.expected_benefit: Faster, deeper research on multi-faceted steps (parallel isolated contexts avoid the single-session context crowding that degrades late-brief quality). Better sub-question separation of concerns — each instance stays focused on one angle.
rejected.risk: Multi-agent runs use ~15x tokens and multiply the end-flush stall surface. Mitigate by pairing with the Workflow-path proposal (stall resilience) and gating the fan-out on a real >=3-sub-question signal so single-question steps stay serial.
rejected.fits_constraints: Parallel INSTANCES of the researcher role, NOT a new role — the project already accepts this reading (researcher.md deep-tier fork). Token cost lands on the Max flat-fee L3 rail, not metered Layer-2. Keeps the 5-file protocol (merged brief is the single research artifact).

verdict.reasoning:
Grounding is real (verified verbatim against the Anthropic multi-agent doc: "3-5 subagents in parallel," "up to 90%" faster, ">10 subagents," "15x more tokens"), and it clears the hard constraints: parallel INSTANCES of the existing researcher role (not a 4th L3 agent, not re-splitting Explore/harness-verifier), Max flat-fee rail ($0 metered), 5-file protocol preserved. So this is not a constraint violation — it's a marginal-benefit-vs-cost REJECT.

Decisive problem: the cited doc undercuts the proposal AT THE COMPLEX TIER. Anthropic conditions multi-agent value on "information that exceeds single context windows" and warns it's not worth it absent that (and where agents must "share the same context"). At complex-tier volume (8-15 full reads, ~25 URLs) on a 1M-context Opus 4.8 at max effort, information does NOT exceed the context window, so the doc's own value criterion is unmet. The deep tier (20-50 sources) is where context crowding actually bites — and the fork ALREADY exists there (researcher.md 242-257, verified). The grounding therefore supports the status quo, not extending fan-out down to complex tier.

Context mismatch: the headline "90% faster" comes from Anthropic's latency-sensitive, breadth-first CONSUMER research product. pyfinagent's research gate is rare-event (once per masterplan step), unattended, single-operator, local — latency is irrelevant. So the primary cited lever does not apply; only the weak depth argument remains, and it's marginal at complex tier.

Risk amplification on a known failure: Agent-tool subagents stalled 6x on 2026-07-11 (model-agnostic; auto-memory feedback_workflow_qa_when_subagents_stall). Tripling parallel researcher spawns triples that stall surface, and the proposal's own mitigation is a SEPARATE unlanded proposal (Workflow-path). Its trigger (partition key = "coverage ledger") is ALSO a separate unlanded proposal. Doubly-dependent on two siblings, it cannot stand alone.

Cost: M implementation (run_harness.py spawns researcher serially/singly via _default_spawn_researcher — fan-out/merge/dedup/origin-labeling is net-new code) plus a permanent maintenance surface, for a marginal depth gain the deep-tier fork already covers. Cheaper status quo: keep the existing deep-tier fork; do not add a complex-tier default. P3 because even reconsidered it's nice-to-have at best.

rejected.title: Codify a context-reset / checkpoint-resume discipline for Main, gated on a real pressure signal (stress-test-doctrine exemplar)
rejected.current_state: Layer-2 has compaction.py + harness_memory.ObservationMasker for in-app context management. Layer-3 Main has the 5-file handoff (which already IS a durable resume artifact) but no codified 'when context pressure is real, checkpoint → /clear → resume from handoff' protocol — and nothing tests whether the assumption 'Main needs compaction/reset at all' still holds on Opus 4.8's 1M window.
rejected.proposed_change: Add a short 'Long-session context discipline' subsection to per-step-protocol.md: (1) prefer RESET over in-place compaction — the 5 handoff files ARE the resume state, so at a real context-pressure signal Main writes a one-line resume pointer, /clears, and resumes from handoff/current + harness_log; (2) explicitly state that on Opus 4.8 (1M) this is expected to RARELY fire, and add a prune-trigger: if it never fires across N sessions, delete the scaffolding. This makes the reset pattern available without prophylactically over-managing context.
rejected.grounding: Anthropic 'Harness design for long-running apps' — 'Context resets were a key unlock... A reset provides a clean slate, at the cost of the handoff artifact having enough state for the next agent to pick up the work cleanly,' plus the caveat that on newer models (Opus 4.6+) 'resets became unnecessary' and 'when a new model lands, it is generally good practice to re-examine a harness, stripping away pieces that are no longer load-bearing' (https://www.anthropic.com/engineering/harness-design-long-running-apps).
rejected.expected_benefit: Gives Main a documented, safe recovery path for genuinely long sessions while explicitly encoding the stress-test doctrine the operator asked us to apply to ourselves — the prune-trigger prevents adding dead scaffolding that Opus 4.8's 1M window may make unnecessary. Reset-over-compaction avoids the 'context anxiety persists' failure the doc attributes to compaction.
rejected.risk: Low. Chief risk is over-building a mechanism that 1M makes moot — which the proposal defuses by gating on a real signal and shipping the prune-trigger. A botched reset could lose in-flight context, mitigated because the 5 files already carry the resume state before any /clear.
rejected.fits_constraints: Uses the existing 5-file handoff as the reset artifact (keeps the durable-state backbone). No new agent, exactly-3 at L3. The prune-trigger is the CLAUDE.md stress-test doctrine applied to the harness itself — exactly the operator's framing. Local-only, Max flat-fee.

verdict.reasoning:
REJECT. Constraint-clean (no new agent, exactly-3 at L3, $0/local-only, no self-eval, no re-split), but fails the leverage and grounding bars.

GROUNDING IS MISATTRIBUTED IN A WAY THAT INVERTS THE ARGUMENT. I fetched the actual Anthropic "Harness design for long-running apps" article. The reset/stress-test quotes are verbatim-real, BUT the load-bearing model claim is wrong: the proposal says "on newer models (Opus 4.6+) resets became unnecessary." The article actually says "Opus 4.5 largely removed that behavior on its own, so I was able to drop context resets from this harness entirely." Attributed to Opus 4.5, and the true statement is STRONGER — Anthropic's own author DROPPED resets entirely. We run Opus 4.8 on 1M, three generations past that point. Read accurately, the cited source is evidence to STRIP context-reset scaffolding, not add it.

ALREADY IMPLEMENTED + AUDITED. The core insight ("the 5 handoff files ARE the resume state; reset > compaction") is already true and documented in three places: docs/audits/compliance-claude-code-surfaces.md P-20 already analyzed checkpointing-vs-handoff and found the handoff protocol provides cross-session resume; docs/archive/RESEARCH_AGENTIC_COORDINATION_LOOP.md already states "Context resets (not compaction) — fresh session with artifact handoff"; docs/runbooks/per-step-protocol.md "Subagent runtime semantics" already codifies partial-work retention + resume-from-artifacts. Every subagent spawn is already a fresh context; Main already resumes from the 5 files + harness_log at session start. There is nothing to "unlock."

SOLE NOVELTY IS MARGINAL-TO-MOOT. The only new element is "Main self-checkpoints and /clears MID-step on a context-pressure signal" — a scenario the proposal itself calls "expected to RARELY fire" on 1M (Main is an orchestrator that offloads heavy context to the researcher/Q/A subagents), with no clear trigger in a headless run_harness.py run. Shipping this rarely-firing mechanism bundled with a "prune-trigger to delete it later" is exactly the stale dead-weight the stress-test doctrine says to strip, added to a must-read-every-session runbook (recurring attention cost). "Add scaffolding + a rule to prune it" is the cargo-cult tell the gate exists to catch. The honest application of the doctrine here is to NOT add it and rely on the handoff-as-resume-state that already exists and that the operator would already use.

rejected.title: Main-session context-compaction discipline anchored on the 5 handoff files
rejected.current_state: There is NO context-management guidance for the long-running Main session. grep for compact/context-rot/context-window in .claude/agents/ and docs/runbooks/per-step-protocol.md returns nothing (backend/agents/compaction.py is Layer-2 in-app only, not Main). Sessions run many cycles back-to-back — harness_log shows Cycles 84→89 in tight succession, and THIS session alone did 67.4 + the ultracode audit + S1/S2/S3 bug fixes — so Main risks auto-compact firing at ~95% mid-GENERATE and silently dropping the in-flight contract from context.
rejected.proposed_change: Add a 'Context hygiene' section to per-step-protocol.md: because the 5 handoff files (not chat history) are the compaction-durable state, Main should run `/compact` WITH a focus instruction at each step's LOG boundary (after the status flip, before the next step's RESEARCH) rather than letting auto-compact fire mid-step. State explicitly that CLAUDE.md + handoff/current/*.md survive compaction while chat history does not, so a mid-step compaction is always recoverable by re-reading the contract. Recommend triggering at natural task boundaries (well before the ~95% auto-trigger).
rejected.grounding: Claude Code compaction system — microcompact / auto-compact / `/compact` with focus at natural task boundaries; 'facts in CLAUDE.md survive compaction; facts in chat history don't' (https://claudelog.com/faqs/what-is-claude-code-auto-compact/ and the Claude Code context-management docs). Directly reinforces Anthropic harness-design's file-based durable-state pattern (https://www.anthropic.com/engineering/harness-design-long-running-apps).
rejected.expected_benefit: Prevents context-rot degradation and mid-step auto-compact surprises on long multi-cycle sessions; makes the file-based handoff the explicit, named recovery anchor after any compaction.
rejected.risk: Very low — a discipline, not code. Over-compaction could drop useful working detail; mitigated by the focus instruction plus the guarantee that re-reading contract.md restores step state.
rejected.fits_constraints: Respects and STRENGTHENS the 5-file protocol (the files are precisely the compaction survivors). No new agents. All other constraints untouched.

verdict.reasoning:
Grounding is REAL and accurately cited (verified against official code.claude.com docs): auto-compaction, microcompaction ("clears stale tool results without calling the model"), and /compact with a focus instruction all exist, and "project-root CLAUDE.md survives compaction; chat history does not" is confirmed verbatim ("after /compact, Claude re-reads it from disk and re-injects it"). So this is not a fake-feature reject. But real grounding is necessary, not sufficient — the proposal fails on merit for three verified reasons:

(1) MECHANISM IS SELF-DEFEATING. It places the guidance in docs/runbooks/per-step-protocol.md. Official docs ("What survives compaction") say ONLY project-root CLAUDE.md and UNSCOPED .claude/rules/*.md are re-injected from disk; an on-demand runbook read into chat history is summarized away like everything else. So the "re-read contract.md to recover" instruction would itself be gone at the moment it's needed. Correct location would be CLAUDE.md or an unscoped rule — not what's proposed.

(2) BENEFIT IS MARGINAL / ALREADY REALIZED. The core insight is already in-repo: RESEARCH.md:258 ("Only project-root CLAUDE.md survives context compaction reliably") and docs/archive/RESEARCH_HARNESS_OPTIMIZATION.md:385-394 (full "Compaction survival checklist": externalize to files, handoff artifacts = step-level state, anti-pattern = state-only-in-conversation). The 5-file protocol plus a compaction-surviving CLAUDE.md (which already carries the entire NON-SKIPPABLE five-file protocol and says the handoff files are durable state) already make the on-disk handoff/current/contract.md recoverable. Auto-compact does not delete the file; Main re-reads handoff/current/*.md as normal course of work. The "silently dropping the in-flight contract" scare is overstated — the contract lives on disk, not only in chat.

(3) MANUAL RITUAL FIGHTS A DOCUMENTED FAILURE MODE. Prescribing "/compact at every LOG boundary" adds one more manual ritual Main is documented to drift on (research gate slipped 7/9 cycles per feedback_research_gate.md; plus contract-before-generate, log-last, status-flip memories). The official memory doc explicitly says point-in-time behaviors belong in a HOOK, not CLAUDE.md prose ("If the instruction is something that must run at a specific point... write it as a hook instead"). Claude Code has PreCompact/PostCompact/SessionStart hooks (project already uses SessionStart) — so the robust build would be a hook, not a drift-prone runbook discipline. Proactive per-boundary compaction also carries a real over-compaction cost (dropping useful cross-step working context the files don't capture), which the proposal itself concedes.

Constraints are all respected (no new agents, $0, local-only, strengthens rather than violates the 5-file backbone) — so this fails on leverage, not on a binding-constraint violation. Net: marginal, partly-redundant benefit delivered via a defective mechanism; maintenance cost (a new drift-prone ritual) exceeds the payoff. If any fragment merited keeping it would be a single recovery-anchor sentence placed in CLAUDE.md (not per-step-protocol.md) with NO per-boundary ritual — a P3 nicety, not the proposal as scoped. Priority set P3 nominally; keep=false.

rejected.current_state: The stall mitigation depends on a human/Main NOTICING a hang: auto-memory feedback_workflow_qa_when_subagents_stall.md says 'watch the subagent transcript mtime (frozen >4-5 min = hung), not the output-file mtime' — but nothing mechanizes this. Pairs with Prop 1 to make the fallback automatic rather than memory-dependent.
rejected.grounding: Claude Code Monitor tool — background monitor that streams events from an mtime/until-loop watch (Monitor tool schema, this session); detection guidance in auto-memory feedback_workflow_qa_when_subagents_stall.md; Anthropic partial-work retention on cutoff (v2.1.199, per-step-protocol.md:270) means the killed spawn's partial artifacts remain readable.
rejected.expected_benefit: Turns 4-day-old tribal knowledge ('watch mtime manually') into a mechanized trip, cutting dead wall-clock on the recurring stalls and making the Workflow fallback reflexive.
rejected.risk: False trips on Opus that is slow-not-hung — mitigated by the ~5-7 min leash from the memo. Monitor only emits an event (report-only); the kill/relaunch stays Main's decision, so no destructive auto-action.
rejected.fits_constraints: No new agents (watchdog observes existing researcher/Q/A spawns). Preserves the 5-file protocol and Q/A independence. Local, $0.

verdict.reasoning:
REJECT. Grounding is real (Monitor tool confirmed; auto-memory mtime guidance verbatim; v2.1.199 partial-work retention at per-step-protocol.md:268-269), but the proposal fails on feasibility and self-consistency.

(1) DIRECT CONTRADICTION with the file it edits: per-step-protocol.md:266-267 — the SAME "Subagent runtime semantics v2.1.198+" section the proposal cites — states "Subagent spawns run in the BACKGROUND by default (v2.1.198); Main is re-invoked on completion. Do not busy-wait or poll transcripts." A Monitor armed on transcript-mtime IS transcript polling. Codifying it would instruct future sessions to do what the protocol bans three lines above the citation.

(2) STRESS-TEST DOCTRINE VIOLATION: v2.1.198 moved spawns to background/re-invoke-on-completion — the runtime is already absorbing this layer. CLAUDE.md's own doctrine says prune scaffolding for what the model/platform now handles; this adds userland scaffolding the platform is evolving to own.

(3) MISSING PRIMITIVE: mechanism hinges on "Main kills the spawn," but no kill-in-flight-subagent primitive exists (TaskStop covers Monitors/background tasks, not Agent-tool spawns). The kill step is hand-waved and incomplete.

(4) NO ATTACHMENT POINT: run_harness.py::_default_spawn_researcher only writes a brief file and returns ("An operator can run the researcher subagent") — no live spawn to watchdog in script mode; manual mode is blocked by (1) and (3).

(5) MARGINAL BENEFIT / FRAGILE TUNING: normal runs are 110-290s; a 5-7 min leash barely exceeds normal runtime, so it saves little wall-clock yet maximizes false trips. Trigger is a one-day cluster (6x on 2026-07-11) — a rare event getting brittle mechanization.

(6) LOW-VALUE LAYER ON UNCODIFIED FOUNDATION: "Proposal 1" (Workflow structured-output fallback) is not yet codified anywhere (grep of qa.md + per-step-protocol.md = nothing). The real value is Prop 1's reactive path, already proven to work manually. This only mechanizes the trigger — the marginal, higher-risk piece.

Net: cargo-cult mechanization of a rare event that contradicts an existing version-grounded rule and needs a primitive that doesn't exist. Keep the reactive Workflow fallback (Prop 1); reject this watchdog.

rejected.title: Enable prompt caching (tools + system, ephemeral 1h) on the Layer-2 MAS Claude orchestrator path
rejected.current_state: multi_agent_orchestrator.py::_get_client() (lines 196-207) returns a RAW anthropic.Anthropic() client. Both _call_agent (line 1008) and _call_agent_with_tools (line 1120, retry at 1232) send system=agent_config.system_prompt as a plain string and tools=AGENT_TOOLS (7 tool defs, lines 71-119) on EVERY tool-loop turn (up to MAX_TOOL_TURNS=5) with NO cache_control. The 4 agent system prompts (each = _QUALITY_CRITERIA + _HARNESS_READ_ONLY + _ANTI_LENIENCY + domain knowledge, well over 1,024 tokens) + the tool array are re-billed at full input price on every turn, every parallel subagent, and every research iteration (up to 3), on every Slack message and per-ticker run_orchestrated_round. The correct pattern already exists in the repo at llm_client.py:1382-1389 (ClaudeClient sends the system block with cache_control ephemeral 1h) but the MAS path does not use ClaudeClient.
rejected.proposed_change: Set cache_control {"type":"ephemeral","ttl":"1h"} on the LAST element of AGENT_TOOLS (and/or pass system as a single-element content-block list with cache_control on the text block). Because the cache prefix order is tools -> system -> messages, one breakpoint on the last tool caches BOTH the tool array and the system prompt. The stable per-agent prefix then registers cache hits from turn 2 onward, across the parallel subagents sharing a prefix, and across messages/tickers within the 1h TTL. Add a debug log of usage.cache_read_input_tokens / cache_creation_input_tokens to verify hit rate (cost_tracker.py already tracks these fields).

Multi-agent research post (https://www.anthropic.com/engineering/multi-agent-research-system): 'token usage by itself explains 80% of the variance' and 'multi-agent systems use about 15x more tokens than chats' -> caching the stable prefix is the single highest-leverage cost lever. Repo's own llm_client.py:1254 documents '~90% cost and ~85% latency' reduction.
rejected.expected_benefit: ~90% cost reduction on the repeated system+tools tokens for the metered MAS Anthropic path (the per-message / per-ticker cost-sensitive path), plus ~85% latency reduction on cached turns. On a COMPLEX query (plan + N subagents x up to 3 iterations + completion checks + synthesize + quality-gate), the shared prefix is re-sent ~10-20x today at full price.
rejected.risk: Low and fail-safe: prefixes under 1,024 tokens silently process uncached (no error). Requires the system prompt/tools to stay byte-stable across calls (they are static module constants). Compose with the structured-output proposal by keeping output_config.format schemas stable (a changing format invalidates the prompt cache).
rejected.fits_constraints: Directly serves 'Layer-2/in-app metered roles stay cost-appropriate' -- this is a pure cost reduction on the metered API path. No cloud infra, no change to the 3-agent Layer-3 harness, no effort bump.

verdict.reasoning:
REJECT. The current-state claims are accurate (verified: backend/agents/multi_agent_orchestrator.py _get_client() returns a raw metered anthropic.Anthropic() at line 204; _call_agent sends system= as a plain string at line 1011; _call_agent_with_tools sends system= + tools=AGENT_TOOLS with no cache_control at lines 1120-1127 and the retry at 1232; 7 tools at lines 71-119; MAX_TOOL_TURNS=5; system prompts are byte-stable f-strings over static constants with no datetime/uuid interpolation; cost_tracker.py tracks cache_read/creation fields at lines 98-99,162-201; the ClaudeClient reference pattern exists at llm_client.py:1387). But the proposal is a silent no-op as written, on a misquoted grounding fact the project already disproved.

DECISIVE MEASUREMENT: the 4 L2 system prompts are all FAR below their per-model cache floors. COMMUNICATION ~721 tok (Sonnet floor 2048); MAIN ~1006 tok (Opus floor 4096); QA ~1070 tok (Opus floor 4096); RESEARCH ~1071 tok (Sonnet floor 2048). Adding the ~400-token tool array does not reach any floor. Below the floor, cache_control silently no-ops: cache_creation_input_tokens stays 0 and the 90% read discount never materializes. So the claimed "~90% cost reduction on the repeated system+tokens" is 90% of a discount that never applies = zero.

GROUNDING MISQUOTE (load-bearing): the proposal asserts "min cacheable = 1,024 tokens on Opus 4.8/Sonnet 4.6." That is false. The authoritative Anthropic prompt-caching doc AND this repo's own llm_client.py:50,1379 state the minimum is 4096 tokens on Opus 4.7/4.8 (and Haiku 4.5) and 2048 on Sonnet 4.6. The 1,024 figure applies only to Sonnet 4.5 and older. This wrong number is exactly what makes the proposal believe its "well over 1,024 token" prompts will cache; with the correct floor they do not. Per the review mandate, a cited claim that does not say what the doc says is a REJECT.

MECHANISM ERROR: the primary instruction ("set cache_control on the LAST element of AGENT_TOOLS ... one breakpoint on the last tool caches BOTH the tool array and the system prompt") is backwards. Prefix order is tools -> system -> messages, so a breakpoint on the last tool caches only the ~400-token tool array; to sweep in the system prompt the breakpoint must be on the last SYSTEM block. Even done correctly it still no-ops at these prompt sizes.

UNACKNOWLEDGED PRIOR ART: the project already hit and solved this exact silent-no-op in phase-25.B9 for the ClaudeClient path (llm_client.py:48-54 documents "the system prompt is ~10-400 tokens and cache_control silently no-ops... the 90% cache_read discount never materializes" and fixes it by prepending a 4500-5000 token _HOUSE_INSTRUCTIONS block to clear the 4096 floor). That comment sits immediately above the very pattern the proposal cites, so the necessary context was in the cited file.

EFFORT/RISK UNDERSTATED: realizing any benefit requires the phase-25.B9-style consolidation (pad each agent prompt above 4096 Opus / 2048 Sonnet), not "S / one line." That padding is then re-sent on every non-reused single-shot call (_call_agent classification/planning/synthesis/quality-gate at line 1008, plus the first turn of every tool loop and every cold-cache call), incurring base input + a 1.25x/2x write premium on tokens that get no reuse. Break-even needs 2-3 reuses per identical prefix per 1h window; on a per-ticker/per-Slack-message path with modest reuse this can be net-negative. The "Low and fail-safe" claim ignores this.

The direction (cache the stable prefix on the metered L2 path) is constraint-respecting and could yield real savings on the tool-loop-heavy turns 2-5 IF re-scoped as a prompt-consolidation + reuse-ratio cost analysis. But as written it would codify a masterplan step that ships a measured zero-benefit no-op justified by a misquoted threshold in an area the codebase already solved. That is a REJECT, not a keep.

Real feature applied to inert code with spec errors = marginal-to-nil benefit against S effort plus new maintenance surface. This is exactly the cargo-cult polish the brief says to reject; the operator's ask is high-leverage Layer-3 harness/MAS upgrades, and this touches an unwired Layer-4 path.
